rows.v: A precise, step-by-step set of instructions for solving a problem or completing a task. The fundamental recipe behind every program.
She optimized the search algorithm to find results in milliseconds instead of seconds.
From the name of 9th-century Persian mathematician al-Khwarizmi, Latinized as 'Algoritmi' — whose work on systematic calculation gave us the term.
A reusable, self-contained block of code that performs a specific task and can be called whenever needed. The basic unit of organizing code.
Instead of repeating the same logic five times, he wrote one function and called it where needed.
Borrowed from mathematics, where a function maps inputs to outputs — programming functions do the same with data, taking arguments and returning results.
A named container that stores a value which can change as a program runs. The basic way code remembers and manipulates data.
He stored the user's score in a variable and updated it each time they earned points.
From mathematics' 'variable' (a quantity that can vary). In code it's literally a labeled box holding a value that may change over time.
A structure that repeats a block of code multiple times — until a condition is met or for each item in a collection. How programs handle repetition.
A simple loop printed every name in the list without writing the same line a hundred times.
From the everyday 'loop' (a circle returning on itself) — the code execution circles back to repeat until told to stop.
Code that makes decisions — running different instructions depending on whether a condition is true or false. The 'if this, then that' logic of programming.
The conditional checked if the user was logged in, showing the dashboard if true and the login page if not.
From 'condition' — the code branches based on conditions. The 'if/else' statement is the most fundamental conditional in nearly every language.
A technique where a function calls itself to solve a problem by breaking it into smaller versions of the same problem. Powerful, elegant, and famously mind-bending.
He solved the folder-scanning task with recursion: the function processed each folder by calling itself on subfolders.
From Latin 'recurrere' (to run back). The classic joke: 'To understand recursion, you must first understand recursion' — a function defined in terms of itself.
Hiding complex details behind a simpler interface, so you can use something without knowing exactly how it works inside. A core principle for managing complexity.
The payment library was a clean abstraction — she called one function and never saw the messy banking details underneath.
From Latin 'abstrahere' (to draw away). It 'draws away' the complicated internals, leaving a simple surface — like driving a car without understanding the engine.
Object-Oriented Programming — a style that organizes code around 'objects' that bundle data and the actions that work on it. One of the dominant programming paradigms.
Using OOP, he modeled each user as an object with its own properties and methods.
An acronym for a paradigm popularized in the 1980s–90s; it organizes programs around 'objects' rather than just sequences of instructions.
An ordered collection of values stored in a single variable, accessed by their position (index). One of the most fundamental data structures.
She stored the seven days in an array and grabbed any one by its index number.
From the everyday 'array' (an ordered arrangement). In most languages the index starts at zero — a famous source of off-by-one confusion.
A data structure that stores values as key-value pairs (also called a dictionary, map, or hash). You look up values by name rather than position.
He used an object to store the user's profile, fetching the email by its key instead of a number.
The 'object/dictionary' name fits: like a dictionary, you look up a value (definition) by its key (word) rather than by position.
A sequence of characters — text. From a single letter to an entire document, any text data is a string.
The function took a string of the user's name and capitalized the first letter.
From the image of characters 'strung' together in sequence, like beads on a thread — one of the oldest data-type names in computing.
A data type with only two possible values: true or false. The foundation of all logic and decision-making in code.
A boolean flag tracked whether the form was valid, flipping to true once all fields passed.
Named after 19th-century mathematician George Boole, whose 'Boolean algebra' of true/false logic became the bedrock of digital computing.
Two ways to organize data by order: a 'stack' is last-in-first-out (like a pile of plates); a 'queue' is first-in-first-out (like a line at a store).
He used a stack to track the undo history and a queue to process incoming jobs in order.
'Stack' from a literal pile (you take from the top); 'queue' from the British word for a waiting line (first to arrive is first served).
Application Programming Interface — a defined way for two pieces of software to talk to each other. How apps request data and services from one another.
The weather app pulled live forecasts by calling a third-party API instead of measuring the sky itself.
An acronym describing the 'interface' through which programs interact — the contract specifying how to ask for something and what you'll get back.
JavaScript Object Notation — a lightweight, human-readable text format for storing and exchanging data. The lingua franca of web APIs.
The API returned the user's data as JSON, which the app parsed into usable objects.
An acronym from its JavaScript origins; its simple, readable structure of keys and values made it the dominant data-exchange format on the web.
An organized collection of data stored so it can be efficiently searched, updated, and managed. The persistent memory behind almost every app.
Every order was saved to the database, so the records survived long after the app was closed.
From 'data' + 'base' (a foundation or store). The structured store that holds an application's information permanently, beyond a single session.
The dominant version-control system, which tracks every change to code over time and lets teams collaborate without overwriting each other's work.
Thanks to Git, she could see exactly who changed which line, and roll back the bug to last week's version.
Created by Linus Torvalds (of Linux fame) in 2005; he jokingly named it after British slang for an unpleasant person — 'I name all my projects after myself.'
A saved snapshot of changes in version control, with a message describing what changed. The basic unit of recorded progress in Git.
He made a commit with the message 'fix login bug' so the team could see exactly what he'd changed.
From 'commit' (to formally record or pledge) — you 'commit' your changes to the project's permanent history.
A separate line of development that lets you work on a feature without affecting the main code; 'merging' combines a branch back in when it's ready.
She created a branch to build the new feature, then merged it into the main code once it was tested.
From the tree metaphor: development 'branches' off from the trunk to grow independently, then merges back — a vivid model of parallel work.
A proposal to merge your code changes into the main project, opened for others to review, discuss, and approve. The heart of team collaboration on platforms like GitHub.
He opened a pull request, and two teammates reviewed his code before it was approved and merged.
Named because you're requesting that maintainers 'pull' your changes into their codebase — the standard gateway for contributing to shared projects.
Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment — automated pipelines that test code as it's added and push it live with minimal manual steps. Modern software delivery.
Their CI/CD pipeline ran every test automatically and deployed passing code to production within minutes.
Acronyms from the DevOps movement; 'continuous' captures the goal of integrating and shipping code constantly rather than in big, risky batches.
The process of finding and fixing errors ('bugs') in code. Often the most time-consuming part of programming.
He spent the afternoon debugging, tracing the crash back to a single misplaced comma.
From removing 'bugs' — a term famously linked to a 1947 incident when a real moth was found jamming a Harvard computer, though 'bug' for a flaw predates it.
Restructuring existing code to make it cleaner, simpler, or faster — without changing what it actually does. Improving the inside, not the behavior.
Before adding features, she refactored the tangled function into three clear, readable pieces.
A compound of 're-' (again) + 'factor' (from mathematics, breaking into components) — reorganizing code's structure while preserving its output.
Short for 'repository' — the storage location for a project's code and its entire change history. Where a codebase lives, often on GitHub.
She cloned the repo to her laptop to start working on the project locally.
A clipping of 'repository' (a place where things are stored), from Latin 'reponere' (to put back). Universally shortened to 'repo' among developers.
'Frontend' is what users see and interact with (the interface); 'backend' is the behind-the-scenes server, logic, and database. Two halves of most web apps.
The frontend showed a sleek button, while the backend quietly processed the payment and updated the database.
Descriptive: the 'front' the user faces versus the 'back' they never see. A 'full-stack' developer works across both.
A pre-built foundation of reusable code and structure that speeds up development by handling common tasks, so you don't start from scratch.
Rather than build everything by hand, they used a web framework that handled routing and security out of the box.
From the literal 'framework' (a supporting structure). It provides the skeleton you build your specific app on top of — scaffolding for code.
HyperText Transfer Protocol — the rules governing how web browsers and servers communicate. 'HTTPS' is its encrypted, secure version.
The browser sent an HTTP request for the page, and the server responded with the HTML.
From its role transferring 'hypertext' (linked documents) on the web; the 'S' in HTTPS stands for 'Secure', added once encryption became essential.
The 'client' (your browser or app) requests something; the 'server' (a remote computer) provides it. The basic two-party model of the internet.
The client app requested today's news, and the server sent back the latest articles.
'Server' from one who serves; 'client' from one who is served. The model mirrors a restaurant — you (client) order, the kitchen (server) delivers.
A temporary store of frequently-used data kept close at hand so it can be retrieved quickly, avoiding slower repeated work. Speeds up nearly everything.
The site cached the homepage so returning visitors loaded it instantly instead of rebuilding it each time.
From French 'cacher' (to hide) — a hidden, fast store of data. Pronounced 'cash'. 'Clearing the cache' is the classic first fix for stale data.
To release code to a live environment where real users can access it — taking a project from a developer's machine to production.
After testing, they deployed the update, and within minutes users worldwide saw the new feature.
From military usage (to position forces into action). In software, you 'deploy' code into live operation — moving it from development into the real world.
An external piece of code or library that your project relies on to function. Modern software is built on towering stacks of dependencies.
The app wouldn't build until she installed a missing dependency the code relied on.
From 'depend' — your code depends on this external piece. 'Dependency hell' is the dreaded tangle when these libraries conflict with one another.
An error or flaw in code that makes it behave incorrectly. The universal name for things gone wrong in software.
A nasty bug crashed the app whenever a user entered an emoji in their name.
Engineers used 'bug' for flaws long before computers; it entered computing lore via a 1947 moth literally stuck in a Harvard machine, which was taped into the logbook.
Standard, repetitive code that must be included in many places with little change — necessary setup that adds bulk but little unique value.
Every new file started with the same twenty lines of boilerplate before the real logic began.
From 19th-century printing, where 'boilerplate' was pre-cast metal text syndicated to newspapers — reused, standardized blocks, exactly as in code.
'Spaghetti code' is tangled, disorganized code that's hard to follow — logic jumping around with no clear structure, like a plate of noodles.
The legacy module was pure spaghetti code, every change risking three new bugs elsewhere.
A vivid metaphor from the 1970s: the control flow is so twisted and interwoven that tracing it is like following a single strand through a plate of spaghetti.
'Rubber-duck debugging' — explaining your code line by line to an inanimate object (like a rubber duck) to find the flaw yourself. The act of explaining reveals the bug.
Stuck for an hour, she explained the function aloud to her desk duck and spotted the error mid-sentence.
From a story in a programming book where a developer carried a rubber duck and debugged by explaining code to it — the act of articulating clarifies the problem.
The future cost of choosing a quick, easy solution now instead of a better one that would take longer. Like financial debt, it accrues 'interest' over time.
Rushing the launch piled up technical debt they spent the next year slowly paying down.
A metaphor coined by programmer Ward Cunningham: shortcuts taken today are a 'debt' that must eventually be repaid, with interest, in extra work.
To release a product or feature to users. A rallying cry in dev culture for getting work out the door rather than endlessly polishing.
The feature was good enough and the deadline loomed, so the lead said 'ship it' and they launched.
From the sense of 'shipping' a finished product. It captures a culture valuing delivery over perfectionism — 'real artists ship', as the saying goes.
